my_sentence: Today I were very busy.
correction: Today I was very busy.
explanation: First-person singular 'I' takes 'was' in the past, not 'were'.
correct_examples: I was very busy yesterday.
I was tired after work.
context: Telling the teacher about his day (daughter had a fever).

my_sentence: She was fine, so we went back to home.
correction: She was fine, so we came back home.
explanation: 'Home' as a destination takes no preposition: 'go/come home', not 'to home'. When returning to where you are, 'come back' is more natural than 'go back'.
correct_examples: I came home late last night.
Let's go home.
context: Saying they left the hospital after his daughter recovered.

my_sentence: If you overspeed on this street, you will be find by the traffic policeman.
correction: If you speed on this street, you will be fined by the traffic policeman.
explanation: In the passive, use the past participle 'fined' (the verb 'fine' = to charge a penalty), not 'find': 'be fined by someone'.
correct_examples: He was fined for parking illegally.
You will be fined if you litter.
context: Making a sentence with the word 'fine'.

my_sentence: They gave me the wrong goods, so we needed to come back to get refund for it.
correction: They gave me the wrong goods, so we needed to come back to get a refund for it.
explanation: The natural phrasing is 'get a refund for it' (or 'a refund on it'). 'Refund' is countable, so use 'a refund', and the preposition is 'for/on', not 'of it'.
correct_examples: I asked for a refund of the ticket price.
Can I get a refund on this?
context: Making a sentence with the word 'refund'.

my_sentence: Recently only my wife and my daughter went to there.
correction: Recently only my wife and my daughter went there.
explanation: 'There' is already an adverb of place, so it takes no preposition: 'went there', not 'went to there'.
correct_examples: We went there last summer.
I'll go there tomorrow.
context: Talking about family vacations to Japan.

my_sentence: It is because we more familiar with it.
correction: It is because we are more familiar with it.
explanation: Every clause needs a verb. With an adjective like 'familiar' you need the verb 'be': 'we are more familiar with it', not 'we more familiar'.
correct_examples: We are familiar with this area.
They are more familiar with the rules than I am.
context: Explaining why his family travels to Japan.

my_sentence: If you feel boring or tired, you can't remember what you learned.
correction: If you feel bored or tired, you can't remember what you learned.
explanation: (assistant note) Use the -ed adjective for how you feel: 'bored'. The '-ing' form ('boring') describes the thing that causes the feeling. 'You are bored' / 'the class is boring'.
correct_examples: I was bored during the meeting.
The movie was boring.
context: Agreeing that learning should be fun.

my_sentence: When I learning something, I want to make the process more interesting.
correction: Whenever I learn something, I want to make the process more interesting.
explanation: Use the present simple 'I learn' after 'whenever/when' (not 'I learning'). 'Whenever' fits a repeated, habitual action.
correct_examples: Whenever I study, I turn off my phone.
When I cook, I listen to music.
context: Talking about how he memorizes things.

my_sentence: If you can't see your monitor clearly, you need to more focus.
correction: If you can't see your monitor clearly, you need to focus more.
explanation: The adverb 'more' goes after the verb it modifies: 'focus more', not 'more focus'.
correct_examples: You should practice more.
I need to sleep more.
context: Discussing eye strain and monitor distance.

my_sentence: Before 2021, every year they need to upgrade their smart glasses.
correction: Before 2021, every year they needed to upgrade their smart glasses.
explanation: The time frame is past ('before 2021'), so use the past 'needed', not 'need'.
correct_examples: Back then, we needed to book tickets early.
Last year they needed more staff.
context: Talking about smart glasses upgrades.

my_sentence: In my country, there are many social media that went bankrupt.
correction: In my country, there are many social media companies that went bankrupt.
explanation: It's the companies, not 'social media' itself, that go bankrupt. Add the head noun: 'social media companies / platforms'.
correct_examples: Several tech companies went bankrupt that year.
Many startups fail in their first year.
context: Talking about social media companies like Tianya going bankrupt.

my_sentence: A millionaire is someone who has a million money.
correction: A millionaire is someone who makes millions / has a million dollars.
explanation: 'Money' is uncountable, so 'a million money' is wrong. Use a unit ('a million dollars') or 'makes millions'.
correct_examples: She makes millions every year.
He has a million dollars in the bank.
context: Defining what a millionaire is.

my_sentence: For my grandfather, he was dead because they were poor.
correction: My grandfather died because his family was poor.
explanation: Two points: use the verb 'died' for the event of dying ('was dead' describes the state afterwards and sounds wrong here); and don't follow the singular 'my grandfather' with an unclear 'they' — say 'his family was poor' (or 'he was poor').
correct_examples: My grandfather died last year.
He died at the age of 80.
context: Talking about relatives who couldn't afford treatment.

my_sentence: She had to stayed at bed every day.
correction: She had to stay in bed every day.
explanation: After 'had to' use the base verb 'stay' (not 'stayed'). Also the set phrase is 'stay in bed' when resting or sick.
correct_examples: I had to stay home yesterday.
He stayed in bed all weekend because he was sick.
context: Talking about a sick relative who couldn't afford treatment.

my_sentence: If you want to get education, you need to spend a lot of money to buy it.
correction: If you want to get an education, you need to pay a lot of tuition fees.
explanation: Say 'get an education' (with 'an'), and you 'pay tuition fees' rather than 'buy' education.
correct_examples: She wants to get a good education.
We have to pay the tuition fees by September.
context: Explaining why people want money (for education).

my_sentence: If you are not at the top level, you can't easy find a suitable job.
correction: If you are not at the top level, you can't easily find a suitable job.
explanation: Use the adverb 'easily' to modify the verb 'find', not the adjective 'easy'.
correct_examples: You can easily find it online.
She easily passed the exam.
context: Talking about the job market for graduates.

my_sentence: It's too difficult find a suitable job in my country.
correction: It's too difficult to find a suitable job in my country.
explanation: (assistant note) 'too + adjective' is followed by 'to + verb': 'too difficult to find'. You drop this 'to' often.
correct_examples: It's too hard to explain.
The box was too heavy to lift.
context: Talking about the difficulty of finding a job.

my_sentence: You are the owner of the company, so you need to consider for your employees.
correction: You are the owner of the company, so you need to consider your employees.
explanation: 'Consider' is transitive and takes a direct object with no preposition: 'consider your employees', not 'consider for'.
correct_examples: A good boss considers his staff.
You should consider other people's feelings.
context: Talking about a business owner's responsibility.

my_sentence: If your company go bankrupt, all of your employees will lose their jobs.
correction: If your company goes bankrupt, all of your employees will lose their jobs.
explanation: The singular subject 'your company' takes 'goes' in the present: 'the company goes bankrupt'.
correct_examples: If the deal falls through, the firm goes under.
If he comes late, we start without him.
context: Talking about employees losing jobs if a company fails.

my_sentence: The government can give them basic necessary even though they go bankrupt.
correction: The government can give them basic necessities even if they go bankrupt.
explanation: Use the noun 'necessities' (the things you need), not the adjective 'necessary'. Use 'even if' for a hypothetical situation, not 'even though' (which states a fact).
correct_examples: The shelter provides basic necessities.
I'll support you even if you fail.
context: Comparing how the US government supports people.
